coredata.dc:title: Nanochannel confined graphene quantum dots/platinum nanoparticles boosts electrochemiluminescence of luminal-O2 system for sensitive immunoassay

coredata.dc:description: Sensitive detection of tumor biomarkers is of great significance for early cancer diagnosis, treatment evaluation, and recurrence monitoring. Development of convenient electrochemiluminescence (ECL) immunosensor using dissolved oxygen (O2) as an endogenous co-reactant of luminol combined with efficient nanocatalysts to boost ECL signal in neutral media is highly desirable. Herein, sensitive detection of tumor biomarker using ECL of luminal-O2 enhanced by confinement of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and platinum nanoparticles (PtNPs) on nanochannel array was demonstrated. A high-density nanochannel array-modified electrode was achieved by rapidly growing an amino-functionalized. Vertically-ordered mesoporous silica film (NH2-VMSF) on the inexpensive and readily available indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode. Through simple electrodeposition, N-GQDs were confined and PtNPs were in-situ synthesized in nanochannels of NH2-VMSF. These confined nanocomposites catalyzed the electroreduction of O2 at negative potentials to generate a large amount of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and facilitated luminol oxidation, enhancing the ECL signal of luminol by 25 times. Two immunosensors were fabricated after covalent immobilization of the recognition antibody of carbohydrate antigen 199 (CA199) or carbohydrate antigen 125 (CA125) on the outer surface of the NH2-VMSF and blocking of non-specific sites. When tumor biomarkers bind to the corresponding antibodies on the recognition interface, the formed immunocomplex hindered the diffusion of luminol to the underlying electrode, resulting in a decrease in the ECL signal and sensitive detection of tumor biomarker. The constructed CA199 immunosensor exhibited a linear detection range for CA199 from 0.5 mU mL−1 to 50 U mL−1, with a detection limit (DL) of 0.03 mU mL−1. For CA125 detection, linear detection ranged from 0.5 mU mL−1 to 500 U mL−1, with a DL of 0.005 mU mL−1. The fabricated immunosensors demonstrated good selectivity and high reproducibility. This study provides great potential for the development of efficient luminol ECL systems in neutral media and expands the biological application in sensitive detection of tumor biomarker.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the fabrication of the immunosensor and the ECL sensing strategy.

(A) Top view TEM image of NH2-VMSF. Inset is the high-resolution TEM image. (B) Cross-sectional SEM image of NH2-VMSF/ITO. (C) Top view SEM image of NH2-VMSF/ITO. (D) Top view SEM image of PtNPs/N-GQDs@NH2-VMSF/ITO electrode fabricated using 0.5s deposition of PtNPs. (E) Top view SEM image of PtNPs/N-GQDs@NH2-VMSF/ITO electrode after 5s deposition of PtNPs. (F) Elemental mapping of Figure E.

(A) XPS survey spectrum of the PtNPs/N-GQDs@NH2-VMSF/ITO electrode. (B) High-resolution Pt 4f spectrum of the PtNPs/N-GQDs@NH2-VMSF/ITO electrode. (C) SEM top-view image of the PtNPs/N-GQDs@NH2-VMSF/ITO electrode after dissolving the nanochannels with NaOH, where the PtNPs deposition time was 0.5 s. The inset shows the corresponding elemental mapping image. (D) Fluorescence image of N-GQDs@NH2-VMSF/ITO. The inset shows the fluorescence microscope image of VMSF/ITO.

(A) ECL signal obtained on different electrodes under continuous scanning in the luminol-dissolved oxygen system. The bias of PMT was 700 V. The CV scan range was from −1.0 V to 0.8 V, with a scan rate of 100 mV/s (B) CV curves of NH2-VMSF/ITO and PtNPs/N-GQDs@NH2-VMSF/ITO in PBS solution under N2 or air atmospheres. The scan rate is 100 mV/s. (C) CV curves of NH2-VMSF/ITO and PtNPs/N-GQDs@NH2-VMSF/ITO in PBS (0.01 M, pH = 7) solution containing different concentrations of luminol under N2 atmosphere, with a scan rate of 100 mV/s. Inset is the enlarged view at a high potential. (D) Relative ratios of ECL intensity in the absence (I 0) and presence (I) of different free radical scavengers. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was obtained in three measurements (n = 3).

(A) CV curves of different electrodes obtained during the fabrication of the immunosensor for detecting CA199 in 2.5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4−, with a scan rate of 50 mV/s. (B) EIS plots obtained on different electrodes fabricated during the construction of the immunosensor and detecting CA199. The electrolyte was 2.5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4−. (C) ECL curves of different electrodes obtained during the fabrication of the immunosensor for detecting CA199 in PBS (0.01 M, pH = 7) solution containing 100 μM luminol. (D) ECL curves of different electrodes obtained during the fabrication of the immunosensor for detecting CA125 in PBS (0.01 M, pH = 7) solution containing 100 μM luminol. For ECL measurement in (C) and (D), the bias of PMT was 700 V. The CV scan range was from −1.0 V to 0.8 V, with a scan rate of 100 mV/s.

(A) ECL signals obtained on the immunosensor after incubation with different concentrations of CA199. (B) Linear fitting curve of the immunosensor for detecting CA199. (C) ECL signals obtained on the immunosensor after incubation with different concentrations of CA125. (D) Linear fitting curve of the immunosensor for detecting CA125. For ECL measurement in (A) and (C), the bias of PMT was 700 V. The CV scan range was from −1.0 V to 0.8 V, with a scan rate of 100 mV/s. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was obtained in three measurements (n = 3).

(A) The quenching ratio of ECL intensity for detecting CA199 in the absence (I 0) or presence (I) of different species. The concentrations of Na+, Lys and Glu are 10 μM, and the concentrations of AFP and CEA are 1 ng mL−1. (B) The ECL signal quenching rate for CA199 (50 mU mL−1) detected by five identical electrodes. (C) The quenching ratio of ECL intensity for detecting CA125 in the absence (I 0) or presence (I) of different species. The concentrations of K+, Na+, and Glu are 10 μM, and the concentrations of AFP and CEA are 1 ng mL−1. (D) ECL signal before or after CA 125 binding and the quenching ratio of ECL intensity for CA125 (0.5 U mL−1) detected by five identical electrodes. For ECL measurement, the bias of PMT was 700 V. The CV scan range was from −1.0 V to 0.8 V, with a scan rate of 100 mV/s. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was obtained in three measurements (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Nanochannel confined graphene quantum dots/platinum nanoparticles boosts electrochemiluminescence of luminal-O2 system for sensitive immunoassay

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensitive detection of tumor biomarkers is of great significance for early cancer diagnosis, treatment evaluation, and recurrence monitoring. Development of convenient electrochemiluminescence (ECL) immunosensor using dissolved oxygen (O2) as an endogenous co-reactant of luminol combined with efficient nanocatalysts to boost ECL signal in neutral media is highly desirable. Herein, sensitive detection of tumor biomarker using ECL of luminal-O2 enhanced by confinement of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and platinum nanoparticles (PtNPs) on nanochannel array was demonstrated. A high-density nanochannel array-modified electrode was achieved by rapidly growing an amino-functionalized. Vertically-ordered mesoporous silica film (NH2-VMSF) on the inexpensive and readily available indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode. Through simple electrodeposition, N-GQDs were confined and PtNPs were in-situ synthesized in nanochannels of NH2-VMSF. These confined nanocomposites catalyzed the electroreduction of O2 at negative potentials to generate a large amount of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and facilitated luminol oxidation, enhancing the ECL signal of luminol by 25 times. Two immunosensors were fabricated after covalent immobilization of the recognition antibody of carbohydrate antigen 199 (CA199) or carbohydrate antigen 125 (CA125) on the outer surface of the NH2-VMSF and blocking of non-specific sites. When tumor biomarkers bind to the corresponding antibodies on the recognition interface, the formed immunocomplex hindered the diffusion of luminol to the underlying electrode, resulting in a decrease in the ECL signal and sensitive detection of tumor biomarker. The constructed CA199 immunosensor exhibited a linear detection range for CA199 from 0.5 mU mL−1 to 50 U mL−1, with a detection limit (DL) of 0.03 mU mL−1. For CA125 detection, linear detection ranged from 0.5 mU mL−1 to 500 U mL−1, with a DL of 0.005 mU mL−1. The fabricated immunosensors demonstrated good selectivity and high reproducibility. This study provides great potential for the development of efficient luminol ECL systems in neutral media and expands the biological application in sensitive detection of tumor biomarker.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pancreatic cancer is one of the most lethal malignancies worldwide. Early detection, diagnosis, and treatment are crucial to controlling the progression of pancreatic cancer and improving survival rates. Tumor biomarkers, produced by tumor cells or tumor-associated tissues, are found at abnormally high levels in the body fluids (e.g. serum) of cancer patients. These biomarkers play important roles in early diagnosis, treatment evaluation, and recurrence monitoring of cancer [1–4]. The primary tumor biomarker used for pancreatic cancer diagnosis is carbohydrate antigen 19-9 (CA199), which belongs to the carbohydrate antigen family and is the most sensitive and specific biomarker for pancreatic cancer [5,6]. Carbohydrate antigen 125 (CA125), also known as mucin 16 (MUC16), is also commonly used as a biomarker for pancreatic cancer [7]. However, the use of a single tumor biomarker for diagnosis can result in false negatives due to the presence of Lewis A antigen negativity [5,6]. The simultaneous detection of multiple tumor biomarkers can effectively avoid false-negative and false-positive results [8]. Therefore, rapid and highly sensitive detection of CA199 and CA125 in serum is of great significance for pancreatic cancer screening, early diagnosis, and treatment monitoring.

In recent years, the development of electrochemiluminescence (ECL) immunosensors for tumor biomarker detection has garnered significant attentions [9–11]. As an analytical method combining electrochemistry and chemiluminescence (CL), ECL involves a luminescent phenomenon occurring at the electrode interface during an electrochemical excitation. Unlike traditional chemiluminescence, ECL is strictly controlled by electrochemical excitation and occur within the diffusion layer at the electrode interface, providing potential for temporal and spatial control and lower background signal [12]. Compared to electrochemical analysis methods, ECL achieves higher sensitivity [13]. However, the automated instruments and specialized reagents for magnetic bead-based ECL immunoassays are usually expensive and require specialized operators. Therefore, developing convenient and cost-effective ECL system for rapid and convenient detection of tumor biomarkers in serum is highly desirable.

ECL system is crucial for the analytical performance of the fabricated immunosensors. Extensively studied ECL emitters include luminol, tris(2,2′-bipyridine)ruthenium(II) (Ru(bpy)3 2+), metal complexes, and nanomaterials [14–16]. Among these, luminol is attractive due to low toxicity, good water solubility, and low cost. However, its quantum yield (1–2%) is relatively low, limiting its applications [17]. By introducing co-reactants to enhance ECL intensity of luminol is widely used currently [18]. Specifically, co-reactants produce highly oxidative or reductive electroactive intermediates during electrochemical reaction. These intermediates undergo high-energy redox reactions with the electroactive intermediates of the ECL emitter, resulting in an excited state that emits photons as it transitions back to the ground state. It has been proven that luminol is electrooxidized to produce luminol anion radicals (L·-) that react with reactive oxygen species (ROS) to reach an excited state, which returns to the ground state with light emission. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is the most commonly used co-reactant in luminol-based ECL systems because it decomposes to produce a large amount of ROS. However, H2O2 is highly unstable at room temperature. Dissolved oxygen (O2) is highly stable, non-toxic, and present in most biological systems, making it an ideal endogenous co-reactant [19,20]. However, the low conversion efficiency of dissolved O2 into ROS and the reduced luminescent efficiency of luminol in neutral media compared to alkaline environments, hinder its practical applications.

The introduction of co-reactant accelerators is effective in enhancing the conversion of dissolved O2 into reactive oxygen species (ROS) [21,22]. Research has proven that certain metal nanomaterials can display catalytic activity [21]. For instance, platinum nanoparticles (PtNPs) can catalyze the electrochemical reduction of oxygen to produce ROS. Additionally, carbon nanomaterials also have demonstrated a sensitizing effect on the ECL response of the luminol-O2 system [23]. Amongst, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are novel graphene-based materials with a size smaller than 100 nm, composed of single or few layers of graphene [24]. GQDs exhibit high stability, large specific surface area, π-π conjugated structure, and low biotoxicity, making them widely applicable in sensing, bioimaging, and other fields [25–28]. By doping heteroatoms into the carbon framework of GQDs, their catalytical properties can be effectively tuned [29–32]. Thus, loading noble metal nanoparticles and doped GQDs onto the electrode surface can act as co-reactant accelerators, enhancing ROS production efficiency and increasing the ECL efficiency of luminol system in neutral medium. However, direct immobilizing nanomaterials on electrodes poses challenges in terms of uniformity and stability. Confinement nanomaterials within porous materials can resolve this problem [33–35]. This approach employes the dispersive space provided by porous materials, increasing the number of active sites and enhancing both catalytic activity and stability [36–38]. In recent years, vertically-ordered mesoporous silica film (VMSF) has been grown on electrode surface using methods such as electrochemically assisted self-assembly (EASA) or Stöber solution growth through inducing the self-assembly of surfactant molecules and the simultaneous hydrolysis/condensation reactions of organosilane molecules [39,41–46]. In addition to high biocompatibility of silica material, VMSF features high-density and mesoscale nanochannels (typically 2–3 nm) with a density up to 8.1 × 1012/cm2, and excellent mechanical and thermal stability [47–49]. Additionally, VMSF is cost-effective and amenable to large-scale batch production. Its two dimensional (2D) ultrathin film structure with adjustable thickness from 20 nm to 200 nm exhibits highly compatible with common flat electrodes, making it an ideal porous material for electrode modification [50–54]. Thus, confining nanocatalysts within the nanochannels of VMSF holds promise for developing highly sensitive luminol-O2 ECL system for the detection of tumor biomarkers.

In this work, two label-free immunosensors were developed based on confinement of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and platinum nanoparticles (PtNPs) within the nanochannels of VMSF, which realized highly sensitive ECL detection of tumor markers including CA199 and CA125. Dissolved oxygen served as an endogenous co-reactant of luminol and PtNPs/N-GQDs act as co-reactant accelerators to boost the ECL of luminol-O2 system. An amino-modified VMSF (NH2-VMSF) was rapidly and conveniently grown on a cost-effective ITO electrode using the EASA method. The amino groups on the surface of NH2-VMSF were derivatized with aldehyde groups for covalent immobilization of CA199 or CA125 antibody (Ab), thus creating two recognition interfaces. Thanks to the excellent catalytic reduction capability of the PtNPs/N-GQDs at negative potentials, the composite can catalyze the O2 to produce a large amount of ROS, thereby enhancing the ECL signal of luminol in neural medium. When the target CA199 or CA125 binds to the corresponding recognition interface, the formed immunocomplex hinders the diffusion of luminol to the underlying electrode, resulting in a decrease in the ECL signal. This mechanism enables highly sensitive detection of CA199 or CA125.

Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) was purchased from Beijing Biosynthesis Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (China). Tumor marker S100 (S100) and alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) were purchased from Beijing Keyu Zhongkai Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (China). Sodium dihydrogen phosphate dihydrate (NaH2PO4·2H2O), disodium hydrogen phosphate dihydrate (Na2HPO4·2H2O), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 98 %), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), glucose, bovine serum albumin (BSA), 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO), luminol, glutamic acid (Glu) and lysine (Lys) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (China). Acetone, anhydrous ethanol (99.8 %), and concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl, 38 %) were purchased from Hangzhou Shuanglin Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) was prepared using Na2HPO4·2H2O and NaH2PO4·2H2O. All chemicals used in the experiments were of analytical grade and were not further purified. The ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ•cm) used in the experiments was prepared by a Mill-Q system (Millipore Corporation). Indium tin oxide (ITO) conductive glass (sheet resistance <17 Ω/sq, ITO thickness: 100 ± 20 nm) was purchased from Zhuhai Kaivo Optoelectronic Technology Co., Ltd (China). Before use, the ITO glass was ultrasonically cleaned in 1 M NaOH aqueous solution for 1 h or soaked overnight, then ultrasonically cleaned in acetone and ethanol for 10 min each, followed by ultrasonic cleaning in ultrapure water for 10 min. The final electrodes were cut and insulated with tape to limit the electrode area to 0.5 cm × 1 cm.

The morphology and thickness of VMSF, as well as the morphology of PtNPs, were characterized using an HT7700 transmission electron microscope (TEM, Hitachi, Japan) and an ULTRA 55 scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss, Germany). For the preparation of TEM sample, the VMSF layer was carefully scraped from the electrode and then ultrasonically dispersed in ethanol. The dispersion was then drop-cast onto a copper grid and naturally dried. Then, the sample was characterized at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. For SEM measurement, NaOH (3 M) was drop-cast onto the electrode surface after nanomaterial confinement (PtNPs/N-GQDs@NH2-VMSF/ITO) to etch the VMSF film. After 5 min, ultrapure water was used to slowly rinse off the surface NaOH. The sample was then naturally dried and investigated using SEM. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed using a PHI5300 spectrometer (PE Ltd, USA) with Mg Kα radiation (250 W, 14 kV). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests were conducted on a CHI660D electrochemical workstation (China). Electrochemiluminescence tests were carried out on an MPI-E II system (Xi'an Remax Analytical Instrument Co., Ltd., China). All experiments were conducted under atmospheric pressure using a three-electrode system with Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) as the reference electrode, platinum wire as the counter electrode, and the modified electrode as the working electrode. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra on different electrodes was perform on CIQTEK EPR 200-Pius spectroscopy (Chinainstru & Quantumtech, China) with continues CV scan (−1∼0.8 V) with a scan rate of 100 mV/s.

NH2-VMSF/ITO was prepared using EASA method [55,56]. Specifically, ethanol (20 mL), NaNO3 (20 mL, 0.1 M, pH = 2.36), CTAB (1.585 g), and APTES (318 μL) were mixed. After CTAB completely dissolved and the solution was uniformly mixed, HCl was used to adjust the solution pH to approximately 2.97. Then, 2732 μL of TEOS was added, and the resulting precursor solution was stirred vigorously for 2.5 h. The pretreated ITO electrode was immersed in the precursor solution and a constant current (−0.7 mA/cm2) was applied for 10 s. After VMSF growth, the electrode was quickly removed, rinsed thoroughly with ultrapure water, and dried with N2. The electrode was then aged overnight in an oven at 120 °C, resulting in an electrode with micelles in the nanochannels named SM@NH2-VMSF/ITO. To remove the micelles, SM@NH2-VMSF/ITO was immersed in a 0.1 M HCl-ethanol solution and stirred for 5 min, yielding the NH2-VMSF/ITO electrode with open nanochannels.

N-GQDs were synthesized using 1-aminopyrene as the precursor under alkaline conditions via a hydrothermal method [57]. Specifically, 1-aminopyrene (2 mg/mL) was added to 20 mL of ammonium hydroxide (0.4 M). The resulting mixture was then placed into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 200 °C for 2 h. The obtained solution was then filtered through a 0.22 μm film to remove large particles, resulting in a reddish-brown solution. This solution was then dialyzed for 2 days using dialysis bags with molecular weight cut-offs of 500 Da and 3500 Da to remove unreacted small molecules and salts formed during the reaction. The dialysate was freeze-dried to obtain solid N-GQDs.

The outer surface of NH2-VMSF was modified by aldehyde groups. Specifically, glutaraldehyde (GA) was used as the crosslinking agent to covalently link antibody on the outer surface of NH2-VMSF. To avoid extensive GA modification on the nanochannels, GA was incubated before removing the micelles. Briefly, SM@NH2-VMSF/ITO electrode was immersed in GA (5 wt%) solution and incubated at 37 °C in the dark for 30 min. The electrode surface was rinsed with PBS (0.01 M, pH 7) to remove excess unbound GA and dried with N2. The resulting GA/SM@NH2-VMSF/ITO electrode was then immersed in an HCl-ethanol (0.1 M) solution and stirred for 5 min to remove the micelles, yielding the GA/NH2-VMSF/ITO electrode. Then, N-GQDs and PtNPs were confined in the nanochannels through electrodeposition and in-situ formation, respectively. For the electrodeposition of N-GQDs, NH2-VMSF/ITO was immersed in a solution of N-GQDs (0.5 mg/mL) and a potential of +0.8 V was applied for 1 min, resulting in the GA/N-GQDs@NH2-VMSF/ITO electrode. The solution used for in-situ PtNPs deposition was H2PtCl6 solution (3.86 mM H2PtCl6 in 0.1 M H2SO4). The prepared GA/N-GQDs@NH2-VMSF/ITO electrode was immersed in the electrodeposition solution, and a constant potential of −0.2 V was applied to form PtNPs within the nanochannels of NH2-VMSF. The resulting electrode was designated as GA/PtNPs/N-GQDs@NH2-VMSF/ITO. Then, GA/PtNPs/N-GQDs@NH2-VMSF/ITO electrode was incubated with CA199 or CA125 antibody (10 μg/mL) at 4 °C for 90 min to immobilize recognitive antibody. The electrode surface was carefully rinsed with PBS (0.01 M, pH 7) to remove unbound antibodies. The resulting electrode was incubated with a BSA solution (1 mg/mL) for 30 min to block non-specific binding sites, followed by rinsing with PBS (0.01 M, pH 7) to remove unbound BSA. The final immunosensor with recognitive interface towards CA199 or CA125, designated as Ab/GA/PtNPs/N-GQDs@NH2-VMSF/ITO, was stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL detection of CA199 or CA125
ce:section.ce:para: To detect CA199 or CA125, the two immunosensors were incubated with the corresponding CA199 or CA125 at different concentrations at 4 °C for 90 min. The electrode was then rinsed with PBS (0.01 M, pH 7) to wash away any unbound CA199 or CA125. The ECL signal of the electrode was measured before and after the binding of CA199 or CA125. The electrolyte solution used for the measurements was PBS (0.01 M, pH 7) containing 100 μM luminol. Continuous CV scanning was employed to trigger the ECL process, with a potential range of −1 to 0.8 V and a scan rate of 100 mV/s. For analysis of real samples, the concentration of CA199 or CA125 in fetal bovine serum was determined using the standard addition method. Prior to measurement, the serum was diluted 50 times with 0.01 M PBS (pH 7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of immunosensor for ECL detection of CA199 or CA125
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 , an immunorecognition interface is constructed on the outer surface of the amino-modified VMSF (NH2-VMSF). For ECL detection, luminol was used as the emitter and dissolved oxygen as the endogenous co-reactant. In addition, PtNPs and N-GQDs confined within the nanochannels act as co-reactant accelerators to build a ternary ECL platform for highly sensitive detection of CA199 or CA125. As shown, indium tin oxide (ITO) conductive glass is employed as the supporting electrode for the fabrication of the immunosensor. ITO is widely used in various electronic devices due to its excellent conductivity and high transmittance. EASA method is used to grow an amino-modified VMSF film on the ITO surface. By adding an amino-functionalized organosiloxane to the precursor solution, amino-modified VMSF can be grown in one step. The mechanism of VMSF growth by the EASA method involves applying a negative voltage or current to the working electrode, forming a pH gradient on the electrode surface through reducing water or hydrogen ions, which further induces the self-assembly of surfactant micelles (SM) on the electrode surface and the hydrolysis/condensation process of the organosiloxane precursor [40]. The SM template can be easily removed by stirring the electrode in a 0.1 M HCl-ethanol solution, ultimately obtaining a film electrode with open nanochannels.

The outer surface of NH2-VMSF provides recognition sites for covalent immobilization of recognitive antibody, constructing a biorecognition interface. The aldehyde derivatization is performed using glutaraldehyde (GA). To prevent GA from entering the nanochannels and affecting mass transfer due to the abundance of amino groups within the channels, GA is attached to the NH2-VMSF surface before the removal of micelles, ensuring that aldehyde functionalization occurs only on the NH2-VMSF surface. Then, N-GQDs are confined into the nanochannels via electrophoresis during electrodeposition. PtNPs are in situ grown in the nanochannels through electrodeposition. Subsequently, CA199 or CA125 antibodies are covalently immobilized, and BSA is used to block non-specific adsorption, constructing the immunosensor.

The PtNPs/N-GQDs confined in nanochannels can act as a co-reactant accelerate for O2 to catalyze the production of a significant amount of ROS, thereby significantly enhancing the ECL intensity of luminol-O2 system.When the target CA199 or CA125 is present in the sample, the corresponding antibodies on the recognition interface can specifically bind to the targets, forming antigen-antibody immunocomplexes. These large immunocomplexes create steric hindrance, obstructing the diffusion of the luminol probe and leading to a decrease in the ECL signal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the morphology of N-GQDs and NH2-VMSF
ce:section.ce:para: The size of the N-GQDs was characterized using TEM. As shown in S1 in the supporting information (SI), the synthesized N-GQDs exhibited good dispersion. N-GQDs had a size ranging from 1.7 to 3.7 nm, with an average size of 2.3 nm ± 0.35 nm (57 samples). The morphology of NH2-VMSF was characterized using TEM and SEM. Fig. 2 A shows a top view TEM image of NH2-VMSF at different magnificence. Inset shows the corresponding high-resolution TEM image. As seen, NH2-VMSF possesses a hexagonally packed pore structure, with nanochannel diameters of approximately 2–3 nm, the corresponding to a porosity of approximately 44 %. The film is continuous and defect-free. Fig. 2B presents the SEM image of cross-sectional of the NH2-VMSF/ITO electrode, clearly showing a three-layer structure corresponding to the glass layer and ITO layer of the ITO electrode, as well as the NH2-VMSF. The thickness of the NH2-VMSF is approximately 88 nm.

To verify the successful confinement of PtNPs, SEM characterization was performed on the electrodes before and after PtNPs deposition. The surface of NH2-VMSF/ITO in Fig. 2C is smooth and defect-free. After depositing N-GQDs for 1 min and PtNPs for 0.5 s on the NH2-VMSF/ITO electrode, the surface remained smooth and defect-free (Fig. 2D), with the morphology similar to that before electrodeposition. When the PtNPs deposition time was appropriately extended to 5 s, a large number of spherical particles of varying sizes were observed on the electrode surface in Fig. 2E. Elemental mapping confirmed these particles to be Pt nanomaterials (Fig. 2F). Therefore, with a short deposition time, PtNPs will be deposited within the nanochannels of NH2-VMSF.

To further confirm the confinement of PtNPs, elemental analysis of the PtNPs/N-GQDs@NH2-VMSF/ITO electrode surface was conducted using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 3 A, characteristic peaks of O, C, N, and Si are observed on the NH2-VMSF/ITO electrode. Specifically, the C 1s, O 1s, Si 2s, and Si 2p3 peaks originate from the silica structure, while the N 1s signal confirms the presence of amino groups in NH2-VMSF. The PtNPs/N-GQDs@NH2-VMSF/ITO electrode exhibits distinct Pt characteristic peaks, with Pt 4f doublets at 71.5 eV and 75.4 eV, indicating the presence of metallic Pt0, thus confirming the confinement of PtNPs in the nanochannels (Fig. 3B). After dissolving the PtNPs/N-GQDs@NH2-VMSF/ITO electrode film in NaOH and observing it with SEM, spherical large particles appear on the electrode surface as shown in Fig. 3C. Elemental mapping shown in inset of Fig. 3C indicates these are Pt particles. This aggregation into larger particles occurs due to the absence of nanochannels for the confinement of smaller PtNPs.

To further confirm the presence of N-GQDs, the fluorescence image of N-GQDs@NH2-VMSF/ITO is shown in Fig. 3D. In compare with NH2-VMSF/ITO (inset of Fig. 3D), the electrode modified with N-GQD displays a distinct fluorescence signal of N-GQDs, demonstrating successful confinement of N-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced ECL signal of luminol-O2 by PtNPs/N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Using dissolved oxygen as an endogenous co-reactant is an effective method for constructing a luminol-based ECL system. As dissolved oxygen is ubiquitously present in the solution, there is no need to add external co-reactants, reducing experimental costs and operational complexity. Fig. 4 A shows the ECL signals measured on different electrodes. It can be observed that due to a porosity of 44 %, the ECL signal on the NH2-VMSF/ITO electrode is lower compared to the ITO electrode. After confining PtNPs/N-GQDs in the nanochannels, the ECL signal of the PtNPs/N-GQDs@NH2-VMSF/ITO electrode is nearly 25 times higher than that of the NH2-VMSF/ITO electrode, and the ECL signal is very stable. As a control, PtNPs/N-GQDs were fixed on ITO using the same method, and the resulting PtNPs/N-GQDs/ITO electrode showed an unstable ECL signal, which was much lower than that of the PtNPs/N-GQDs@NH2-VMSF/ITO electrode. These results demonstrate that confining PtNPs and N-GQDs in nanochannels can significantly enhance the ECL signal of the luminol-dissolved oxygen system. As shown in Fig. 4B, the PtNPs/N-GQDs@NH2-VMSF/ITO electrode exhibits a distinct O2 reduction peak (∼−0.4 V) in the presence of O2, indicating that the PtNPs/N-GQDs has significant catalytic reduction capability for O2. Thus, this work uses dissolved oxygen as the co-reactant for luminol and PtNPs/N-GQDs as the co-reactant promoter to enhance the ECL signal. Specifically, O2 is first electrochemically reduced by PtNPs/N-GQDs at a negative potential, generating a large amount of reactive oxygen species (ROS), thereby increasing the ECL efficiency of luminol and consequently enhancing the ECL signal. When luminol is present in the system, as shown in Fig. 4C, a noticeable luminol oxidation current is observed around 0.8 V. The current value indicates that PtNPs/N-GQDs nanocomposites also catalyses luminol oxidation compared to NH2-VMSF/ITO.

The effects of solution pH and temperature on the ECL signal of PtNPs/N-GQDs@NH2-VMSF/ITO electrode have been investigated (Fig. S2 in SI). As shown in Fig. S2A, the ECL signal is low in acidic medium, while higher ECL signals are observed under neutral or alkaline conditions. Generally, in the absence of a catalyst, the luminol-dissolved oxygen system exhibits a stronger signal under alkaline conditions, while the ECL signal is lower in acidic or neutral conditions [21]. This is attributed to the fact that in an alkaline environment, luminol more readily undergoes deprotonation to form luminol anions, and dissolved oxygen also more easily generates reactive oxygen species (ROS). However, when a nanocatalyst is confined within the nanochannels, a high ECL signal is observed even under neutral conditions due to its ability to catalyze the electrochemical oxidation of luminol and the electrochemical reduction of oxygen. Considering that serum samples have a near-neutral environment, pH 7 was selected for subsequent studies. As shown in Fig. S2B, the ECL signal increases as the temperature rises from 4 °C to room temperature, which can be attributed to the enhanced reaction rate at higher temperatures. However, as the temperature continues to rise, the ECL signal decreases, primarily due to the thermal decomposition of luminol, the reduced concentration of dissolved oxygen, and the increased occurrence of side reactions, all of which negatively affect the luminescence efficiency. Thus, measurement at room temperature was chosen for further investigates.

The types of ROS produced during the reaction were investigated through radical scavenging experiments (Fig. 4D). The ECL signal significantly decreased upon adding the superoxide radical (O2 ·-) scavenger p-benzoquinone (BQ). However, adding the hydroxyl radical (·OH) scavenger tert-butanol (TBA) only reduced the ECL signal by about 18 %. Thus, the main ROS present in the system is O2 ·-, with some ·OH. This indicates that PtNPs catalyze the electroreduction of O2, producing a large amount of O2 ·- and further generating ·OH to promote the ECL of luminol. The primary ROS generated during the ECL process was further confirmed using electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy. Continuous cyclic voltammetry (CV) scans were conducted on the PtNPs/N-GQDs@NH2-VMSF/ITO electrode within a potential range of −1 V–0.8 V in a 90 % methanol-water solution with in situ ESR measurements. As shown in Fig. S3 (SI), the DMPO-O2 •- adduct displayed a characteristic 1:1:1:1 peak, indicating the presence of O2 •-. In contrast, the NH2-VMSF/ITO electrode without the confined nanomaterial exhibited no obvious O2 •- signal. These results suggest the presence of O2 •- during the ECL process and demonstrate the catalytic effect of the confined nanomaterial on ROS generation. The ECL reaction mechanism is as follows:

Specifically, luminol loses a proton to become the luminol anion (LH−). During the process of electrochemical oxidation, it loses an electron to form the luminol anion radical (L•−), and the composite material PtNPs/N-GQDs further promotes the oxidation of LH−. O2 is first electrochemically reduced to superoxide anion radicals (O2 ·−) by PtNPs/N-GQDs at a negative potential. Additionally, due to the excellent catalytic reduction properties of PtNPs/N-GQDs, O2 is further reduced to H2O2. The reaction between superoxide anion radicals and H2O2 generates highly oxidative OH·. The luminol anion radical (L•−) reacts with superoxide anion radicals and hydroxyl radicals (ROS) to produce the excited state luminol (AP2−∗). When it returns to the ground state 3-aminophthalate (AP2−), ECL emission (hv) occurs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of PtNPs and N-GQDs deposition conditions
ce:section.ce:para: To enhance the sensitivity, the deposition times of PtNPs and N-GQDs were optimized. As shown in Figs. S4A and B, when the deposition time is too short, the catalytic activity of the material is low, resulting in a lower ECL signal. However, if the deposition time is too long, it affects the mass transfer of luminol molecules, also leading to a decreased signal. Ultimately, an electrophoresis time of 1 min for N-GQDs and a deposition time of 0.5 s for PtNPs were selected for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility and optimization of immunosensor construction
ce:section.ce:para: The stepwise modification process for the fabrication of immunosensor was verified using CV and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). As shown in Fig. 5 A and B, after confining PtNPs and N-GQDs within the nanochannels, the CV signal of the GA/PtNPs/N-GQDs@NH2-VMSF/ITO electrode significantly increased compared to GA/NH2-VMSF/ITO, and the relative impedance decreased. This is attributed to the enhanced conductivity provided by the nanomaterials, particularly PtNPs. As the sensor undergoes stepwise modifications, the introduction of antibodies and BSA blocking leads to a gradual decrease in the electrode's CV signal and a corresponding increase in impedance. Fig. 5C and D also show a progressive reduction in ECL response. This is due to the insulating properties and large size of proteins such as antibodies and BSA, which increase surface resistance and hinder the diffusion of luminol to the electrode. When the immunosensor is incubated with CA199 or CA125, the ECL signal of the electrode significantly decreases because of the specific recognition between antigen and the recognitive antibody. This immunocomplex also increases interface resistance, due to its large structure, further reduces the diffusion of luminol and the supporting electrolyte solution to the electrode surface, thereby lowering the ECL signal.

To ensure the immunosensor constructed achieves optimal detection performance, the construction parameters for the fabrication of sensor were optimized, including antibody immobilization time as well as the incubation time for the target binding. Specifically, GA/PtNPs/N-GQDs@NH2-VMSF/ITO was incubated with anti-CA199 or anti-CA125 antibodies to construct two recognition interfaces. As shown in Fig. S5A, the ECL signal of luminol gradually decreased as the concentration of anti-CA199 increased until the antibody concentration reached 10 μg/mL, after which the signal plateaued. The resulting immunosensor exhibited a high ECL signal quenching ratio, where quenching ratio was calculated using ECL signal of electrode before (I 0) and after (I) target incubation (I 0 – I/I 0, also ΔI/I 0)). This is likely due to the saturation of antibody binding on the aldehyde surface at 10 μg/mL antibody. The incubation time for the target analyte CA199 was also optimized. As shown in Fig. S5B, the ECL response of the electrode gradually decreased with increasing incubation time until it stabilized at 120 min, indicating the binding of CA199 reached saturation, with the highest quenching ratio observed. Similarly, the incubation conditions for detecting CA125 were optimized. Figs. S5C and S5D show the ECL signal of electrode after different immobilization of CA125 antibody and different incubation time for CA125 binding, with both reaching saturation at 90 min. These optimized conditions were used in subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL detection of CA199 and CA125
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal experimental conditions, the performance of the constructed immunosensor for ECL detection of CA199 and CA125 was investigated (Fig. 6 ). Fig. 6A and C shows the ECL signals of the two immunosensor after incubation with different concentrations of CA199 or CA125, respectively. It can be observed that the ECL signal of the electrode gradually decreases as the concentration of CA199 and CA125 increases. This is attributed to the formation of immunocomplexes between the antibody and the corresponding antigen, which hinders the diffusion of luminol to the supporting electrode. When the concentration of CA199 varies between 0.5 mU mL−1 and 50 U mL−1, the quenching ratio of ECL intensity (ΔI/I 0) has a linear relationship with the logarithmic value of CA199 concentration (logC CA199) (ΔI/I 0 = 0.128logC CA199 + 0.248, R 2 = 0.998, Fig. 6B). The detection limit (DL), calculated based on a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (S/N = 3), is 0.03 mU mL−1. Table S1 (in SI) demonstrates comparison between performance for CA 199 detection using different methods [58–63]. The DL is lower than that obtained using electrochemical detection (EC) based on sandwich immunoassay using PtRu-polylysine-the secondary antibody/CA199/BSA/the primary antibody/gold nanospheres modified glassy carbon electrode (PtRu-PL-Ab2/CA199/BSA/Ab1/AuNSs/GCE) [58] or nano-silicon sphere-cyclodextrin-the secondary antibody/CA199/BSA/the primary antibody/Au nanoparticles/reduced graphene oxide modified GCE (nSiO2-CD-Ab2/CA199/BSA/Ab1/AuNPs/rGO/GCE) [59] or Au@CuxOS yolk-shell nanostructures with porous shells-the secondary antibody/CA199/BSA/the primary antibody/AuNPs/rGO-tetraethylene pentaamine modified GCE (Au@CuxOS-Ab2/CA199/BSA/Ab1/AuNPs/rGO-TEPA/GCE) [61], or EC based on a 3D microfluidic electrochemical origami immunodevice (μ-ECOI) [60] or CA199/BSA/Ab/bimetallic alloyed AuAg hollow nanocrystals modified GCE (CA199/BSA/Ab/AuAg HNCs/GCE) [63], or terahertz-timedomain spectroscopy detection using CA199/BSA/Ab/AuNPs modified terahertz substrate (CA199/BSA/Ab/AuNPs/THz substrate) [62].

Similarly, when the concentration of CA125 varies between 0.5 mU mL−1 and 500 U mL−1, the quenching ratio of ECL intensity has a linear relationship with the logarithmic value of CA125 concentration (logC CA125) (ΔI/I 0 = 0.102logC CA125 + 0.189, R 2 = 0.990, Fig. 6D), with a DL of 0.04 mU mL−1. Table S2 (in SI) demonstrates comparison between performance for CA 125 detection using different methods [64–69]. The DL is lower than that obtained using ECL detection based on graphitic carbon nitride coated with Fe3O4 nanoparticles (nanoFe3O4@g-C3N4) modified screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPCEs) [64] or Au nanoclusters-11-mercaptoundecanoic acid/the secondary antibody/CA125-the primary antibody-p-aminobenzoic acid modified GCE (AuNCs-MUA/Ab2/CA125/Ab1-ABA/GCE) [67] or TiO2 nanoparticles-carbon nanotubes-chitosan/CdSe nanocluster-the secondary antibody/CA125/Ab1/CNTs modified GCE (TiO2 NPs-CNTs-CS/CdSe NCs -Ab2/CA125/Ab1/CNTs/GCE) [68], or multiplex lateral flow immunoassay (MLFIA) based on biotin-enriched dendritic mesoporous silica nanoparticles [65], or FL detection using antifouling magnetic beads/aptamer 1/CA125/aptamer 2/yellow colored carbon dots (MBs/Apt1/CA125/Apt2/y-CD) probe [66]; or EC detection using the secondary antibody-O-succinyl-chitosan-magnetic nanoparticles-toluidine blue/CA125/BSA/the primary antibody/three-dimensional reduced graphene oxide-multiwall carbon nanotubes-polyamidoamine/gold nanoparticles modified GCE (Ab2-Suc-CS@MNP-TB/CA125/BSA/Ab1/3DrGO-MWCNT-PAMAM/AuNPs/GCE) [69].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and reproducibility of the immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity and reproducibility of the sensors were investigated (Fig. 7 ). To study the selectivity of the constructed immunosensor, tumor markers such as alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) and carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), along with substances commonly found in serum such as glucose (Glu), ascorbic acid (AA), Na+ ion, and two common amino acids in the human body including glutamic acid (Glu) and lysine (Lys) were selected as potential interferents. The changes in the ECL signal of the electrode in the presence of these substances were examined. As shown in Fig. 7A, when CA199 is absent in the detection sample, ECL signal of the sensor remained essentially unchanged. However, when the recognition antibody is incubated with CA199 or a mixture containing CA199, the ECL signal significantly decreases, and the reduction value is similar. Fig. 7C shows that CA125 also exhibited similar selectivity. These results indicate that the fabricated sensor has specific recognition capabilities for the target analytes CA199 and CA125 and is not easily affected by other coexisting substances, demonstrating the high selectivity of the constructed immunosensor.

To investigate the reproducibility of the fabricated immunosensor, five identical electrodes were prepared in parallel and used to detect the target analyte. As shown in Fig. 7B, the five electrodes prepared under the same conditions were used to detect CA199, and the quenching ratios of their ECL signals were almost identical, with a relative standard derivation (RSD) of only 1.8 %. As shown in Fig. 7D, if the ECL response of the immunosensor without incubating the antigen or the electrode after binding with CA125 is used as the detection signal, the RSD for the five sensors is 4.8 % or 4.6 %, respectively. However, if the quenching rate of ECL signal is used for detection, the RSD is only 1.9 %. Thus, using the quenching rate of the ECL signal before and after binding as the detection signal can reduce errors.

To study the detection reliability of the prepared immunosensor, the two immunosensors with CA 199 or CA125 recognition interface were used to determine CA199 and CA125 in fetal bovine serum (FBS) by the standard addition method. As shown in Table 1 , different concentrations of CA199 standard solution were added to FBS sample followed with the 50-fold dilution. For CA 199 detection, the measured recovery ranges from 99.7 % to 100 % with RSD not exceeding 1.31 %. For CA125, the measured recoveries ranges from 94.7 % to 103 %, with RSD no more than 0.79 %. These results demonstrate high detection accuracy of the constructed immunosensors and their potential for tumor marker detection.

In this study, luminol was used as the ECL emitter, with dissolved oxygen serving as an endogenous co-reactant. N-doped graphene quantum dots and platinum nanoparticles confined in nanochannels were employed as co-reactant accelerators to construct a signal-enhanced ECL system. After constructing the immunorecognition interface, the developed immunosensor achieved highly sensitive detection of tumor markers CA199 and CA125. The supporting electrode of the construction of immunosensor was ITO conductive glass, which is inexpensive, easy to pattern, and miniaturize. The high-density, amino-modified nanochannel array film can be quickly prepared in one step. N-GQDs were confined within the nanochannels and platinum nanoparticles were in-situ prepared in the nanochannels by electrodeposition. Both N-GQDs and PtNPs efficiently catalyzed the electroreduction of dissolved O2 at negative potentials and promoted luminol oxidation, generating a large amount of ROS, thus enhancing the ECL signal of luminol. After covalently immobilizing the recognition antibody on the outer surface of the NH2-VMSF, the immunosensor could be conveniently fabricated. The constructed immunosensor demonstrated high sensitivity, good selectivity, stable signals and high reproducibility for detecting CA199 or CA125.
